turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch also has the opinion of the court this morning in case 17-387, Upper Skagit Indian Tribe v. Lundgren.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: This case comes to us from the Supreme Court of Washington. It began when Sharline and Ray Lundgren sued the Upper Skagit Indian Tribe to resolve which of them owns a strip of land that borders their adjoining properties. The Tribe asserted sovereign immunity and sought dismissal of a suit, but the Supreme Court of Washington interpreted this Court's 1992 decision, County of Yakima v. Confederated Tribes and Bands of Yakima Nation, to deprive Indian tribes of their sovereign immunity and so-called in rem lawsuits over property, like this one. Lower courts have disagreed about whether Yakima has this effect, so we took the case to clear the confusion. We now hold that the state Supreme Court was mistaken. I will spare you the details, which I promise will bore you. It is enough to say here that Yakima had nothing to do with sovereign immunity, instead it was a prosaic statutory interpretation case concerning, yes, the tax consequences of Section 6 of the General Allotment Act of 1887. Having cleared that much up, we vacate the judgment of the state Supreme Court and remand the case for further proceedings. That Court should address in the first instance the Lundgren's alternative argument that the immovable property exception to sovereign immunity applies to allow the suit here to proceed. The Chief Justice has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Kennedy has joined. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Alito has joined.
